SAP S/4HANA helps reduce this fragmentation by acting as a shared operational environment for the whole company. Instead of every department using separate systems and isolated workflows, they all use the same operational framework. SAP integration allows areas that depend heavily on each other to exist inside one connected environment with information being consistently shared across the whole organization.

I came across a situation like this some years ago, when we worked with a global company that supports energy and field operations around the world. This company had a very large amount of operational purchasing activity, but many of the SAP processes behind it were way too manual compared to the size of the business. When the company contacted us, they had already went through problems during maintenance shutdowns because a lot of sites were sending urgent material requests at the same time through SAP.

To help with this, we automated part of the SAP purchasing and approval process so urgent requests could move faster and teams didn’t have to spend so much time manually checking and coordinating everything. This helped them process materials faster during maintenance periods and reduced a lot of repetitive admin work between operations and procurement teams.

And that is my point: SAP S/4HANA, at least in the Oil and Gas environment, is the easiest and safest way of connecting processes in real time. One of the biggest operational pressures in Oil and Gas environments comes from the fact that many activities cannot simply “wait until tomorrow.” Maintenance schedules, field operations, procurement cycles, and inventory availability are often directly connected to production continuity. Even the smallest delay can affect the whole system in these kinds of fields.

One thing is clear: operational efficiency is not only related to production itself. In fact, if production goes well it’s because there is something else functioning behind it, and that has to do with how quickly companies are able to react when conditions change. This is one of the reasons why SAP S/4HANA has become increasingly important in asset-intensive industries. It allows organizations to build more connected operational environments where information can move faster between teams and operational areas. In industries where downtime, delays, or lack of visibility can quickly translate into operational and financial consequences, having more standardized and integrated processes becomes less of a technological improvement and more of an operational necessity.

Conclusion

In Oil & Gas, circumstances almost never slow down enough to permit a full reset. Teams have to keep the wheels turning as production can’t stop, maintenance issues pop up, materials need to be in the right place, and decisions have to be made fast, all under constant stress. Minor flaws in how companies do things can snowball into much bigger problems if no one catches them early.

That’s where SAP S/4HANA really shows its value. Sure, it covers all the basics, but it goes further: it helps companies build smoother, more connected operations. Processes speed up, data gets more reliable, and teams don’t have to waste so much time tangled in paperwork or chasing down info. In a fast-paced world like Oil & Gas, being able to respond quickly and stay consistent isn’t just nice to have, it’s essential.

entry.title: Location Master Data Replication: SAP TM to Business Network for Logistics
entry.content:
Location master data is one of the key master data objects in Transportation Management and Freight Collaboration within Business Network for Logistics. Proper location master data management ensures accurate mapping between Business Network for Logistics and Transportation Management systems, enabling seamless transportation processes and freight collaboration

Problem Statement

  • Manual creation limitations: Creating location master data manually in Freight Collaboration is not feasible when dealing with large numbers of location records
  • Maintenance difficulties with large datasets: Manual maintenance becomes difficult and error-prone as the volume of location master records increases
  • Naming convention consistency requirements: Location master codes must be identical in both Freight Collaboration and Transportation Management to ensure proper mapping between systems

Recommended Solution

Implementing the Data Replication Framework (DRF) provides automated replication of location master data from TM to Freight Collaboration, eliminating manual creation challenges and ensuring data consistency across systems

Location is a logical or physical place in which products or resources are managed on a quantity basis.

You use this business object as the basis for transportation processes. In transportation processes, you name a source location, a destination location, and any transshipment locations necessary to complete the transportation process. For this purpose, you define locations.

Tcode - /SCMTMS/LOC3

Manage location app is used to manage location master data, which was manually copied or replicated from SAP S/4HANA or created from new using the app Location master data can be used by other apps, such as Manage Entities, in SAP Business Network Freight Collaboration.

We need to create same location master data in SAP Business Network Freight Collaboration which is exist in SAP TM means the naming convention must be same.

Hence it is very difficult to create manually location master data one by one. Hence it would be better to replicate the master data automatically in SAP Business Network Freight Collaboration.

Master Data Replication in SAP TM - Configuration : 

To set up master data replication, you must configure the replication of master data via the Data Replication Framework (DRF) in the SAP S/4HANA source system. DRF uses SOAP service - LocationBulkReplicationRequest to replicate location master data from the source system to SAP Business Network for Logistics.

Tcode – DRFIMG

Define Custom Settings for Data Replication > Define Technical Settings > Define Technical Settings for Business Systems

Create a business system using your LBN ID

Select the newly created business system and navigate to Define Bus. System, BOs in the dialog structure.

Enter 189 as the BO type and Location as the description.

Enter 189 as the BO type and Location as the description.

Create a new communication channel called Replication via Services

Tcode – DRFIMG

Define Custom Settings for Data Replication > Define Replication Models

Create a replication model.

Select the newly created replication model and choose Assign Outbound Implementation.

Select the outbound implementation 189_1 and choose Assign Target Systems for Repl. Model / Outb. Impl to assign the target system (identified by its LBN ID – as maintained in the business system) to the outbound implementation.

Optional: If you only want to replicate one default language description, select the outbound implementation and navigate to Assign Language

Choose Activate

Tcode – DRFF (Optional Step)

In the worklist, search for the replication model name you have created in step 2, and define the filter criteria for including or excluding business objects

Tcode – SOAMANAGER

Activate consumer proxy: /SCMB/CO_CPX_LOC_BULK_REPL_REQ . Create a logical port and set it as the default port.

TCODE – DRFOUT

Select the newly created replication model and select the following settings under Replication Mode:

  • To send an update of all locations, select Initialization.

  • To send the lasted changed location, select Changes.

  • If you have defined your own filter criteria, select Manual. The manual replication will either be based on the filter criteria defined in step 3, or be based on the filter that you manually maintained in the DRFOUT transaction.

entry.title: Visibility Is Already There: Stop Overengineering Your Yard in SAP EWM
entry.content:
Visibility Is Already There: Stop Overengineering Your Yard!

Manyat Thinking: Simplicity is the Ultimate Sophistication

Introduction

In many SAP EWM projects, teams often struggle with yard visibility. When a truck arrives, the first reaction is usually:  “We can’t see what’s inside the truck.” And from there, the solution becomes:
          . Create new storage types
          . Create yard bins
          .  Add additional Warehouse Tasks
          . Build extra tracking logic
But what if the visibility already exists in SAP EWM — and we are simply not using it correctly ?

The Trap of Overengineering

To solve visibility problems, projects often introduce:
                  . Logical yard bins for each truck
                   . Extra Warehouse Tasks only for visibility
                   . Additional stock movements for tracking
Initially, this feels like better control. However, over time it creates:
                    . More system load
                    . Slower warehouse execution
                    . Additional process complexity
                   .  Unnecessary maintenance effort
You start with a visibility problem and end up creating an execution problem.

The Manyat Approach: Visibility Already Exists

SAP EWM already provides a natural visibility chain:

Transportation Unit (TU) → Delivery → Material
This already tells us:
               . Which truck has arrived
               . Which deliveries belong to the truck
               . Which materials and quantities are expected
So the real question becomes:
If the information already exists, why are we recreating it through additional stock movements?

Using Existing Data for Smart Yard Planning

SAP EWM is already integrated with SAP TM and delivery processes. When a truck arrives:
        The Transportation Unit is linked to a Delivery
        The Delivery already contains material and quantity information
        Expected stock is already visible in the system

Using standard monitoring tools such as /SCWM/MON, teams can:
          . Track deliveries linked to a TU
         .  Identify expected material and quantities
         .  Match incoming stock with warehouse requirements
          . Prepare labor and resources in advance
        . Plan door assignments earlier
Planning can also be improved using:
          . Delivery priority
         . ETA and waiting time
         . Queue and sequencing logic
There is no need to create artificial stock just to “see” visibility.

A Shift in Thinking

Traditional thinking often looks like this:
Truck → Staging → Planning → Execution
But a smarter approach is:
Truck → Yard Planning → Staging → Execution
This small shift changes a lot. It allows warehouses to:
          . Reduce unnecessary warehouse movements
          . Improve planning accuracy
          . Reduce system load
         .  Execute operations faster
Most importantly, planning happens before execution bottlenecks appear.

Key Insight

For planning activities, expected data is often enough.
Actual confirmation should happen during execution — not during planning.
Creating additional warehouse movements only for visibility is not optimization.
It is overengineering.

Conclusion

A smart warehouse design is not about adding more configuration.
It is about understanding what already exists in SAP EWM and using it correctly.
The visibility was always there.
We just needed to look at it differently.

By following this visibility approach, warehouse teams can reduce confusion, avoid unnecessary movements, improve truck planning, and achieve better coordination between yard and warehouse operations.

Instead of recreating visibility again and again, the focus should be on using the existing visibility effectively for faster and more controlled execution.

How do you currently manage yard visibility and truck planning in your warehouse operations? I would love to hear different real-world approaches and experiences from the community.


— Manyat Thinking
Part of Manyat Project – SAP EWM Simplified By Rahul Kumar

entry.title: New E-learning: Implementing SAP S/4HANA for Advanced Available-to-Promise
entry.content:
Is keeping delivery promises a challenge in your supply chain?

SAP S/4HANA for advanced available-to-promise (ATP), also known as Advanced Available-to-Promise (aATP), is designed to address exactly that. It evaluates supply and demand in real time to ensure accurate and dependable order confirmation dates, while protecting your business priorities and profitability goals. Built natively into the SAP S/4HANA core, it works seamlessly across all deployment options: cloud or on-premise.

Introducing the New E-learning 

Unlock the potential of SAP S/4HANA for advanced ATP with our e-learning:

Implementing SAP S/4HANA for Advanced Available-to-Promise

Through this course, you will:

  • Get comprehensive guidance on implementing SAP S/4HANA for advanced ATP to optimize order promising processes.
  • Explore core aATP capabilities, configure supply protection and product availability checks, and apply best practices for delivering reliable customer commitments in real-time while balancing supply chain constraints and business priorities.
  • Configure and implement SAP S/4HANA for advanced ATP capabilities to deliver accurate order commitments while managing supply chain constraints and business priorities.

Ready to get started? Dive into the e-learning experience now!

Note: Access to this e-learning requires a one-time registration to SAP Learning.

 

Enhance Your SAP Experience with Value Maps

Beyond e-learnings, SAP Enterprise Support offers additional resources to help you drive innovation. Value maps on SAP Community bring together guidance, recommended actions, and supporting resources to help you decide what to focus on next - all included in your SAP cloud subscription.

👉  Watch our short demo video, then head over to our page to explore our range of topics and request a call with a topic expert for guidance tailored to your goals.

 

About the author: 
Mabelle Roque Miguel is a Topic Expert for SAP Enterprise Support value maps, specializing in SAP Supply Chain Management solutions. Based in Singapore, Mabelle has been with SAP since 2007, taking on diverse roles that have largely centered around customer engagement and success. Now, her focus is on guiding businesses through their supply chain journey, helping them accelerate the adoption of SAP supply chain management solutions with value map content and SAP Enterprise Support offerings.

  Have questions or feedback around this content? Feel free to leave a comment below or contact @MabelleRM.

entry.title: 3-Level Advanced Intercompany Stock Transfer - Technical Deep DIve
entry.content:
READ PART 1 FIRST — FUNCTIONAL OVERVIEW

This document is the technical continuation of the functional blog published on SAP Community: "3-Level Advanced Intercompany Stock Transfer" (https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/3-level-advanced-intercompany-stock-transfer/ba-p/14150304). If you haven't read that one yet, it's worth doing — it covers the business case, the process flow, and the Financial Chain concept that everything here is built on.

This is the first of two technical posts. It walks through the architecture and custom development that drives the order creation part of the 3-Level STO process — the VCM configuration, the Financial Chain plumbing, the value chain steps, and the ABAP classes we built to handle them. 

 

Introduction

Before we dive in, a quick recap of the problem. SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS2 Private Cloud Edition ships Advanced ICo Stock Transfer as a two-company process — one delivering plant, one receiving plant, done. But, we need three. There's a Receiving Company that raises the Stock Transport Order, a Delivering Company that physically ships the goods, and an Intermediate Company sitting in the middle that handles the legal and financial leg between the two.

We couldn't just flip a configuration switch to get there — the standard framework simply doesn't support it. So we built on top of it. The design principle we held to throughout: no modifications to core Value Chain Framework internals, all custom logic isolated so it only fires for the 3-Level STO scenario, and the implementation stays close enough to SAP's own product direction that upgrading later won't be a nightmare.

The Three Legal Entities

Each company owns a distinct set of documents in the value chain:

ENTITY

DOCUMENTS IN VALUE CHAIN

Receiving Company (RC)

Stock Transport Order (ZRC_STO), IC Purchase Order (ZRC_PO), Inbound Delivery, GR to storage location, ICo Supplier Invoice (MIRO)

Intermediate Company (IC)

ICo Sales Order (ZIC_SO), ICo Purchase Order (ZIC_PO), Goods Receipt in Transit Plant, Goods Transfer to SIT, Goods Issue from SIT, ICo Customer Invoice, ICo Supplier Invoice

Delivering Company (DC)

ICo Sales Order (ZDC_SO), Outbound Delivery (ZDC_OD), Goods Transfer to SIT (ZDC_GT), Goods Issue from SIT (ZDC_GI), ICo Customer Invoice

 

The Intermediate Company never physically handles the goods. It exists purely in the financial and legal layer — it buys from the Delivering Company on paper, sells to the Receiving Company on paper, and the goods go straight from one physical plant to the other. That's the whole point of having a Financial Chain rather than trying to model this as two consecutive 2-Level STOs.

 

  1. VCM Configuration Objects

The first thing we did was carve out a separate configuration namespace for the 3-Level STO. A dedicated chain category (ZIT3) and chain type (ZIC_STOCKTRANSFER_3L) mean the two processes are cleanly independent from the VCM perspective.

OBJECT TYPE

TCODE / PATH

VALUE

DESCRIPTION

VCM Chain Category

VCM_CHAIN_CAT

ZIT3

3-Level Advanced Intercompany Stock Transfer — the top-level grouping for our custom process

VCM Chain Type

VCM_CHAIN_TYPE

ZIC_STOCKTRANSFER_3L

Full value chain definition holding all 18 steps and their handler class assignments

Purchase Doc. Type

MM Customizing

ZBIC

References standard UB; assigned to category ZIT3. This is what the user creates in ME21N.

Sales Order Type

VCM Assignment

ZAIS

Assigned to ZIT3 with Process Relevance set to Delivering / Intermediate Company

Item Category

VCM Assignment

CBIC

Used for Sales and Stock Transfer items under ZIT3

Step Handler ZRC_STO

VCM_VC_CHAIN_TYPE

ZCL_PO_IC_VCMS_INIT_STO

Our custom handler registered for the initiating STO step

Step Handler ZRC_PO

VCM_VC_CHAIN_TYPE

ZCL_PO_IC_VCMS_STO2PO

Our custom handler for the IC PO creation step in Receiving Company

Step Handlers — GMs

VCM_VC_CHAIN_TYPE

ZCL_MMIM_STEP_HANDLER_3L

Shared goods movement handler registered for ZDC_GI, ZIC_GR, and ZIC_GT steps

 

  1. Financial Chain

If you read the functional blog, you already know what a Financial Chain is conceptually. Here's what it looks like from a code perspective.

Every STO item that qualifies for 3-Level processing gets a Financial Chain ID assigned to it — stored in the custom field ZZFIN_CHAIN_ID. That ID tells you exactly which legal entities are involved, what the transit plant is, and what the delivering plant is. From that point on, every downstream document copies the ID from its predecessor. If it's missing anywhere in the chain, something went wrong.

Financial Chain Path

ENTITY

ROLE

PLANT

Delivering Company

Ships goods physically

Delivering Plant

Intermediate Company

Legal / paper entity only — no physical goods

Transit Plant

Receiving Company

Final stock destination

Receiving Plant

 

BAdI: Financial Chain Determination for STO Items

The actual determination of which Financial Chain applies to a given STO item happens via a BAdI in our case.

ZZFIN_CHAIN_ID Propagation Map

Once the Financial Chain ID is stamped on the STO item, it has to travel cleanly through every document that follows. We appended ZZFIN_CHAIN_ID to each relevant structure and set up implicit enhancements to copy it forward at each step transition. The rule is simple: wherever VCM creates a new document from a predecessor, the ID comes with it.

DOCUMENT

STRUCTURE / TABLE

MECHANISM

Stock Transport Order

EKPODATA, MEPOITEM_DATA

Structure append; BAdI populates the field when the STO is saved

ICo Sales Order Items

VBAP (custom append)

Implicit enhancement ZCL_IM_SD_ADD_FCID_IN_SO at end of IF_SD_SLS_IC_SOTOPO~SO_DATA_TO_PO_DATA

ICo Purchase Order Items

MMPUR_S_PO_IC_STO_CHG_ITEM — append ZAMMPUR_S_PO_IC_STO_CHG_ITEM

Implicit enhancement ZCL_IM_MM_ADD_FCID_IN_PO in GENERATE_NEW_PO_ITEM_DATA; reads FC ID from VBAP via the predecessor SO item

Outbound Delivery Items

OD item structure

Copied from STO item by the VCM step handler during OD creation

Material Documents

Material document item

Each goods movement step handler copies from its predecessor step

Supplier Invoice (MIRO)

EK08RZ append ZAEK08RZ; RSEG append ZARSEG

B2B interface enhancement; copied from the corresponding ICo PO item

 

  1. Value Chain Step Configuration

The chain type ZIC_STOCKTRANSFER_3L is where the full process gets wired up. TCode – VCM_CHAIN_TYPE

STEP

DESCRIPTION

TYPE

PREDECESSOR

ZRC_STO

Receiving Company — Stock Transport Order (Initiating)

Initiating

—

ZRC_PO

Receiving Company — IC Purchase Order (to IC)

Synchronous

ZRC_STO

ZIC_SO

Intermediate Company — IC Sales Order (from RC)

Synchronous

ZRC_PO

ZIC_PO

Intermediate Company — IC Purchase Order (to DC)

Synchronous

ZIC_SO

ZDC_SO

Delivering Company — IC Sales Order (from IC)

Synchronous

ZIC_PO

ZDC_OD

Delivering Company — Outbound Delivery

External

ZRC_STO (precond: ZDC_SO, ZIC_PO, ZIC_SO, ZRC_PO)

ZDC_GT

Delivering Company — Goods Transfer (Stor. Loc → SIT)

External

ZDC_OD

ZDC_GI

Delivering Company — Goods Issue from SIT

Synchronous

ZDC_GT

ZIC_GR

Intermediate Company — Goods Receipt in Transit Plant

Synchronous

ZDC_GI

ZRC_ID

Receiving Company — Inbound Delivery

Synchronous

ZDC_OD

ZIC_GT

Intermediate Company — Goods Transfer (Transit → SIT)

Synchronous

ZIC_GR

ZIC_GI

Intermediate Company — Goods Issue from SIT

Synchronous

ZIC_GT

ZRC_GR

Receiving Company — Goods Receipt in Transit Plant

Synchronous

ZIC_GI

ZRC_GT

Receiving Company — Goods Transfer (SIT → Storage Loc.)

External

ZRC_ID

ZDC_CI

Delivering Company — ICo Customer Invoice

External

ZDC_OD

ZIC_CI

Intermediate Company — ICo Customer Invoice

External

ZDC_OD

ZIC_SI

Intermediate Company — ICo Supplier Invoice

External (B2B)

ZIC_GR

ZRC_SI

Receiving Company — ICo Supplier Invoice

External (B2B)

ZRC_GR

 

  1. VCM Step Handler Classes

The classes below were created new or enhanced for the individual VCM steps mentioned. For the remaining steps of the value chain, we used the standard classes of 2 level STO value chain steps.

ZCL_PO_IC_VCMS_INIT_STO

Step Handler — ZRC_STO (Initiating Step)

Handles the initiating STO step. Its main job is filtering: before VCM processes items downstream, this class checks that each STO item actually has confirmed quantities. Items without confirmed qty that are also new to the IC PO in the receiving company get filtered out — they're not ready yet.

Key Methods

▸  IF_VCM_STEP_HANDLER~FILTER_OUTBOUND_ITEMS — main filter logic

▸  READ_PURCHASE_ORDER — reads STO data via CL_MM_PUR_PO_IC_FACTORY

▸  HAS_CONFIRMED_QUANTITY — checks for at least one schedule line with confirmed qty

▸  IS_ITEM_NEW — checks whether the item already has a corresponding IC PO item

▸  INITIALIZE_BUFFERS — clears instance buffers at the start of each run

 

 

ZCL_PO_IC_VCMS_STO2PO

Step Handler — ZRC_PO (IC Purchase Order in Receiving Company)

Orchestrates the creation of the ICo Purchase Order in the Receiving Company. It groups the inbound bundle items, delegates to the synchronizer class, and maps the results back to VCM as an outbound bundle. Think of it as the coordinator — the actual PO logic lives in the synchronizer and POM classes below.

Key Methods

▸  IF_VCM_STEP_HANDLER~EXECUTE — called once per inbound bundle group; kicks off PO creation

▸  IF_VCM_STEP_HANDLER~GROUP_INBOUND_ITEMS — groups STO items into bundle groups before EXECUTE

▸  IF_VCM_STEP_HANDLER~GET_EXECUTABLE_ITEMS — propagates completed items to the next step

▸  GET_ITEMS_FOR_IC_PO — extracts STO item keys from the inbound bundle

 

 

ZCL_PO_IC_SYNC_STO2PO

Singleton Synchronizer

This is the singleton that actually manages the grouping of STO items into PO objects and drives the create/update logic. It keeps a buffer (G_TAB_POM_ORDERS) of PO objects across items so that multiple STO items that belong to the same IC PO get handled in one go.

Key Methods

▸  GET_INSTANCE (static) — returns the singleton instance for the current LUW

▸  GROUP_INBOUND_ITEMS — creates ZCL_PO_IC_POM_ITEM objects and groups them into PO objects

▸  CREATE_PO_FROM_STO — fetches the PO object for a given bundle group and calls SYNCHRONIZE

▸  ADD_ITEM_TO_PO — adds an item to an existing or newly created ZCL_PO_IC_POM_ORDER object

▸  GET_EXISTING_IC_PO_FOR_STO — looks up existing IC POs for update scenarios

▸  PRELOAD_POM_ORDERS — pre-loads existing IC PO objects into the buffer for change handling

 

 

ZCL_PO_IC_POM_ORDER

PO Object Model — Order

Implements IF_MM_PUR_PO_IC_POM_ORDER. Represents a single ICo Purchase Order being built or updated. On create, it calls CREATE_PURCHASE_ORDER from the standard DAO CL_MM_PUR_PO_IC_DAO_BUILD_POH. On update, it uses GET_DATA_FOR_CHANGE and UPDATE_PURCHASE_ORDER from the same DAO. Supplier and goods supplier are determined by calling ZCL_ICSTO_HELPER.

Key Methods

▸  IF_MM_PUR_PO_IC_POM_ORDER~SYNCHRONIZE — entry point; branches to create or update path

▸  IF_MM_PUR_PO_IC_POM_ORDER~SET_VCM_CHAIN_CATEGORY — sets ZIT3 on the PO object

▸  GENERATE_NEW_IC_PO_DATA — builds PO header and calls GENERATE_NEW_IC_PO_ITEM_DATA per item

▸  MERGE_NEW_AND_OLD_IC_PO_DATA — applies latest STO changes to an existing IC PO's builder data

▸  GET_PO_ITEM_NUMBER_INTERVAL — returns item number interval based on purchasing document type

 

 

ZCL_PO_IC_POM_ITEM

PO Object Model — Item

Implements IF_MM_PUR_PO_IC_POM_ITEM. One object per IC PO item being created. It reads the STO item data (via CL_MM_PUR_PO_IC_FACTORY), picks up the Financial Chain ID from the STO item, and builds the IC PO item payload. The split criteria it calculates — STO doc number + FC ID + Sales Org + Division + Distribution Channel — determines which items end up in the same IC PO.

Key Methods

▸  SET_STO_REFERENCE — sets target operation and calls READ_STO_ITEM_DATA

▸  READ_STO_ITEM_DATA — fetches STO data via factory class; maps ZZFIN_CHAIN_ID

▸  DETERMINE_SPLIT_CRITERIA — calculates the grouping key for IC PO item assignment

▸  IF_MM_PUR_PO_IC_POM_ITEM~GET_ITEM_DATA_FOR_UPDATE — applies latest STO changes to existing IC PO item

▸  DETERMINE_PO_ITEM — finds the existing IC PO item number for an STO item in update scenarios

▸  MAP_ITEM_DATA_TO_PORDER_ITEM — maps STO item fields to IC PO builder structure

▸  MAP_SCHEDULE_TO_PORDER_ITEM — maps STO schedule line data to IC PO schedule lines

 

 

ZCL_ICSTO_HELPER

Helper Class

Central utility class for 3-Level STO operations. Determines supplier and goods supplier from the Financial Chain ID — this is where the lookup into the financial chain definition happens to find the correct business partner numbers. Also provides the CHECK_IS_VCM_CATEGORY_ZIT3 method that all the standard enhancements call to decide whether to apply 3-Level logic.

Key Methods

▸  DETERMINE_SUPPLIER — derives supplier for IC PO from the Financial Chain definition

▸  DETERMINE_GOODS_SUPPLIER — derives goods supplier (always the physical delivering plant) from FC

▸  CHECK_IS_VCM_CATEGORY_ZIT3 — returns true if the PO item's VCM chain category is ZIT3

▸  READ_BILLDOC — fetches customer invoice data and checks 3-Level sales / STO relevance

▸  ENHANCE_GR_INFORMATION — enriches invoice item data with GR details for B2B invoice linking

▸  PERFORM_3L_IC_CHECK_BEFORE_CRT — validates invoice data before creation in the B2B flow

▸  CHECK_CREDIT_MEMO_RELEVANCE — checks credit memo applicability for reversal documents

▸  PROCESS_INVC_AFTER_APPL — handles VCM integration after the invoice application interface

 

 

ZCL_MMIM_STEP_HANDLER_3L

Material Document Step Handler

Common handler for all the goods movement steps. Rather than writing a separate handler class for each goods movement step, we used one class with dedicated PREPARE_* methods per step. This class is shared with the 3-Level Advanced ICo Sales value chain too — the logic for preparing a GR document is the same regardless of whether the initiating document was a Sales Order or a Stock Transport Order.

Key Methods

▸  PREPARE_IC_GI — prepares Goods Issue from Delivering Company SIT (ZDC_GI)

▸  PREPARE_IC_GR — prepares Goods Receipt in Intermediate Company Transit Plant (ZIC_GR)

▸  PREPARE_IC_GT — prepares Goods Transfer in Intermediate Company Transit Plant to SIT (ZIC_GT)

▸  PREPARE_RC_GR — prepares Goods Receipt in Receiving Company Transit Plant (ZRC_GR)

 

 

  1. SAP Standard Enhancements

 

Class Enhancements on Standard VCM Classes

STANDARD CLASS

ENHANCEMENT

WHAT IT DOES

CL_MM_PUR_PO_IC_VCMI_STO

Class enh. ZCL_PO_IC_VCMI_STO
New methods: CREATE_VCM_PROCESS_3L, ADD_NEW_ITEM_TO_VCM_CREATE_3L

Groups STO items by FC ID and creates a separate VCM instance per Financial Chain. This is what makes one STO → multiple VCM chains possible.

CL_MM_PUR_PO_IC_VCMI_OUT

Class enh. ZCL_PO_IC_VCMI_OUT
New static buffers: G_TAB_3L_VCM_PROCESS_DATA, G_TAB_3L_VCM_UPDATE_DATA, G_TAB_3L_VCM_DELETE_DATA

Holds 3-Level-specific VCM process data across create/update/delete operations. Buffers are cleared via a pre-exit on INITIALIZE.

CL_MM_PUR_PO_IC_VCMI_ADAPTER

Spots ZSP_START_VALUE_CHAIN, ZSP_CREATE_VALUE_CHAIN
Enh. on START_VALUE_CHAIN, CHANGE_VALUE_CHAIN

Fetches VCM instance GUID after chain creation and persists it to Z_VCMCHAIN_INS. Also supplies the correct GUID for delta items during change operations.

CL_MM_PUR_PO_IC_VCMI_STO

Methods: TRIGGER_VCM_PROCESS_FROM_BADI, GET_ITEMS_FOR_VCM_UPDATE, ADD_UPDATE_ITEM_IF_RELEVANT, GET_ITEMS_FOR_VCM_DELETE

Extended to handle create/update/delete flows for 3-Level; items are tagged with the appropriate operation (CREATE / UPDATE / DELETE) for downstream VCM processing.

CL_LE_SHP_ICO_VCM_STEP_IDRC

Impl. ZCL_LE_SHP_ICO_VCM_STEP_IDRC, ZCL_LE_SHP_RETRY_VCM_CHAINS

Maps VCM data for the inbound delivery creation step; handles retry of related VCM chains when the inbound delivery is processed for 3-Level STO.

CL_MM_PUR_PO_IC_VCMI_FACTORY

Enh. ZCL_MM_PUR_PO_IC_VCMI_FACTOR on CREATE_VCMI_FILTER

Sets the correct delivery class instance for the 3-Level STO scenario.

CL_SDBIL_VCM_HANDLER

Enh. ZSP_3L_SDBIL_VCM_HANDLER on IF_SDBIL_VCM_HANDLER~CHECK_CANCELLATION

Ensures the step type is determined correctly during intercompany invoice cancellation.

CL_MMIV_IC_BADI_VCM_APP_CANCEL

Enh. ZSP_IS_CANCELLABLE on IS_CANCELLABLE

Returns true when the step type is ZIC_SI, ZSC_SI, or ZRC_SI — enabling cancellation of 3-Level supplier invoice steps.

 

Include and Function Module Enhancements

OBJECT

ENHANCEMENT SPOT / IMPL.

WHAT IT DOES

Include LEINRF2I

ZSP_LEINRF2I

Sets VCM chain category to ZIT3 on outbound delivery GI posting

Include LV50SF10

ZSP_3L_STO_UPDATE_VCM_CAT

Sets correct VCM step types based on chain category during delivery processing

Include LEINRF2C

ZSP_LEINRF2C

Populates Financial Chain ID during PGR confirmation search

Include LEINRF2A

ZSP_LEINRF2A

Fetches the correct document during PGR for 3-Level STO

Include MM07MFB7_BESTELLUNG_WA_PRUEFEN

ZSP_BESTELLUNG_WA_PRUFEN_03

Skips a standard error message during GI for STO when the chain category is ZIT3

Include MM07MFB7_BESTELLUNG_PRUEFEN

ZSP_BESTELLUNG_PRUEFEN

Skips error M7-E011 if the VCM chain category is ZIT3; checked via ZCL_ICSTO_HELPER~CHECK_IS_VCM_CATEGORY_ZIT3

Include MM07MFB7_BESTELLUNG_UEBERNEH02

ZSP_BESTELLUNG_UEBERNEH02

Clears the receipt indicator (kzzug) for movement type 107 when category is ZIT3 — needed for GR in the inbound delivery to post correctly

FM MEX_PSTYP_KNTTP

ZSP_MEX_PSTYP_KNTTP

Skips the account assignment error for 3-Level STO items — the account assignment category combination is different for this process and would otherwise cause a false error

FM ME_CONFIRMATION_SEARCH_GR

ZSP_ME_CONFIRMATION_SEARCH_G

Populates Financial Chain ID during GR confirmation search for supplier invoice confirmation

 

COMING UP IN PART 3

The second technical post covers the runtime side of the process: how the Goods Movement steps are executed and timed using Transfer of Control Dates, why we switched to B2B interfaces for Supplier Invoice instead of letting VCM trigger it, how the change and delete scenarios work at the code level.

entry.title: Are You Chasing "Ghost Stock"? The Reality Check Your Warehouse Needs
entry.content:
Are You Chasing "Ghost Stock"? The Reality Check Your Warehouse Needs

Manyat Thinking: Simplicity is the Ultimate Sophistication

Introduction

In my day-to-day experience managing warehouse operations and handling logistics flows, nothing frustrates an execution team more than a system showing stock that physically doesn't exist on the floor.

You check the bin… empty.
You check the system… it insists the stock is right there.

We all know this operational headache as Ghost Stock. Whenever I discuss these visibility discrepancies with warehouse managers, the instant reaction is often to blame an SAP bug or ask for a complex system enhancement. But if you look at it through the lens of "Manyat Thinking"—where we believe simplicity is always better than over-engineering—the issue is rarely a configuration failure. It is almost always a gap in the physical movement process.

The Shopkeeper Paradox: Understanding Ghost Stock

To understand why Ghost Stock occurs even in a massive automated warehouse, we don't need to look at complex system architecture first. We just need to look at a local shopkeeper.

Imagine a shopkeeper who receives a fresh shipment of goods. On Day 1, he does a physical count, verifies everything, and stores the items on his shelves. But a few days later, during a routine stock check, he notices a mismatch. The physical items on the shelf do not match what he thinks should be there. Suddenly, he is dealing with "Ghost Stock"—inventory that exists in his mind or old notes, but is physically missing from the shelf.

What does he do to solve this? He doesn't panic; he executes a manual supply chain audit:

- He checks the original invoice to see what actually entered his shop. (In SAP EWM, this is your PRDI — Product Inbound Delivery).
- He reviews his pending and confirmed orders to see what was moved or misplaced within the shop. (In SAP EWM, this is your ORDIM_C — Warehouse Task/Confirmation).
- He tracks what has already been sold and packed for the customers. (In SAP EWM, this is your PRDO — Product Outbound Delivery).

By matching these three points, the shopkeeper instantly finds his mistake. He realizes exactly which stock was pending, which was confirmed, and which had already left the shop.

The biggest realization here is this: Whether it is a small retail shopkeeper or a 500,000 sq. ft. automated distribution center running SAP EWM, the foundational logic of supply chain visibility remains exactly the same. Ghost stock isn't a system glitch; it is a breakdown in tracking the execution journey between Inbound, Internal Movement, and Outbound.

The Reconciliation Logic: Follow the Flow

Instead of just staring at system screens or waiting for custom reports, the real solution lies in understanding exactly how data flows across the execution chain. If you look at the reconciliation diagram I attached with this post, you can see how we map warehouse behavior across those same three critical checkpoints.

1. /SCWM/PRDI — Planned Expectation
An Inbound Delivery is not just a document. It represents the expected movement.
- What material is supposed to arrive
- How much quantity is expected
This is the starting point of warehouse visibility.

2. /SCWM/ORDIM_C — Execution Reality
The truth lies in confirmed Warehouse Tasks.
- Confirmed tasks represent actual execution
- Unconfirmed tasks indicate incomplete movement
For example, if 10 units were expected but only 8 were confirmed, those remaining 2 units were never fully processed in the system. This is where inconsistencies begin.

3. /SCWM/PRDO — Final Result
Outbound Delivery reflects what actually left the warehouse.
- What was picked
- What was issued
Any mismatch here exposes hidden process issues.

The Manyat Formula for Clarity
Inbound = Intent
Tasks = Execution
Outbound = Result

When these three layers remain aligned, warehouse visibility stays accurate.

Gap Analysis: Where the Ghost Is Created
Ghost Stock is usually created because of:
- Wrong postings
- Incomplete confirmations
- Process gaps
- Data inconsistencies

In most cases, the issue is not the stock itself. The issue is the break in the movement chain.

Key Insight: Practical Action Playbook for Reducing Ghost Stock

Theoretical understanding is important, but operational control is what actually reduces Ghost Stock in day-to-day warehouse execution. Below is a simple operational approach that warehouse teams can use to identify and reduce visibility gaps.

1. Daily Triad Validation
Perform a quick comparison between /SCWM/PRDI (Expected), /SCWM/ORDIM_C (Confirmed), and /SCWM/PRDO (Issued). Any mismatch between these three layers should immediately be reviewed delivery-wise. This helps identify visibility breaks before they grow into larger inventory inconsistencies.

2. Define Clear Exception Buckets
Warehouse teams should classify mismatches into clear categories such as:
- Short Confirmation (Expected quantity > Confirmed quantity)
- Over Confirmation (Confirmed quantity > Expected quantity)
- Issue Gap (Confirmed quantity ≠ Issued quantity)
Once these categories are standardized, corrective actions become much faster and more consistent.

3. Root Cause Classification
Each mismatch should be tagged with a clear operational reason:
- Incomplete Warehouse Task
- Wrong bin posting
- Queue or timing delay
- User execution error
Over time, these classifications reveal recurring operational patterns.

4. Fast Correction Process
A simple correction workflow can significantly reduce Ghost Stock duration:
- Open Warehouse Tasks → Complete or cancel appropriately
- Bin mismatch → Recount and adjust inventory
- Outbound gap → Reconcile picking and goods issue activities
Speed of correction is critical for maintaining warehouse visibility accuracy.

5. Prevention Controls
Strong process controls reduce future inconsistencies:
- RF validations for quantity, batch, and bin
- Mandatory task confirmations
- Cut-off validations before Goods Issue
- Additional checks for high-value materials
Prevention is always more effective than repeated correction.

6. Simple KPI Monitoring
Operational visibility improves when warehouses track measurable indicators such as:
- Mismatch percentage between PRDI and ORDIM_C
- Issue gap percentage between ORDIM_C and PRDO
- Average resolution time for mismatches

Ghost Stock is not a system failure. It is a visibility issue created by incomplete movement execution. Where movement stops, visibility becomes unreliable.

My Personal Take: Movement Over Screens

In managing and designing warehouse flows, I firmly believe that technology, systems, and even AI should support smarter operational decisions—not simply automate a weak, messy, or overcomplicated process.

When you synchronize your yard visibility, staging logic, and cross-docking properly, complexity drops naturally and your inventory stays clean.

In SAP EWM, stock is not just a static quantity. It is about movement, confirmation, and control.

Conclusion

Counting stock is easy. Understanding how that stock moved is what defines a real warehouse professional. Because in SAP EWM, stock is not just about quantity. It is about movement, confirmation, and control. And where movement becomes incomplete, Ghost Stock begins to appear.

I would also love to hear how different warehouse teams identify and control Ghost Stock situations in real-world operations. Let’s share some practical fixes in the comments.

— Rahul Kumar
Manyat Thinking | Part of Manyat Project – SAP EWM Simplified
